item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get it, it's an often-heard complaint. Glad you liked the steampunk goggles. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's too much left unwritten in this one for me. It almost certainly comes down to personal taste but I prefer a long winded but informative scip that answers a lot of my questions while also making me think of some more questions than a short article that makes me go but what about xyz and leaves me with a bunch of unanswered questions. Much as it pains me to downvote a scip that mentions steampunk goggles I feel I have to.

item.title: Re: Image source
item.content:encoded: Because of this, the image has been removed from the article.

item.title: Image source
item.content:encoded:
As per Tineye, these goggles were available for sale on SRA-Solder.com in September 2012, a good year before this article as written. We can reasonably infer that it is their original source.

SRA-Solder has a reasonably comprehensive copyright notice at the bottom; it includes their images.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: If we can find a commonality in another article, something involving a Christian sect fighting demons, I'd be happy to modify this article to fit it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Those would make more sense, structurally, if they were included in the table rather than in the body of the text.

You could also add some otherwise invisible sapient entities whose souls might be made visible in testing.

Upthread, Crayne hints that this John guy is significant, but I can't figure out where those hints lead, are there possible cross-references there?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Works for me. :)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
If that is the case, then any SCP object which contains a soul would have its own orb, would it not? Things like SCP-1875 and the test objects of SCP-158.

As such, lemme provide the following proposal:

Subjects that do not remove an instance of SCP-1344, report seeing orbs with an average estimated diameter of 8.7 cm, and of varying colouration, hovering in place over the heads of both the majority of human beings and other mammals subjects are exposed to. These orbs move with the persons or animals in question. Tests with various species of insects, arachnids, fish, amphibians, reptiles and birds have yielded no results. It is unknown why this phenomenon is only reported with the majority of human beings as opposed to all other mammals. It is noted that multiple SCP objects have been observed to have orbs of their own as well, including SCP-1875 and the products of SCP-158 testing. Research into the exact criteria for the manifestation of this phenomenon is ongoing.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As the author, I'm going to have to veto this edit. The orbs witnessed through SCP-1344 instances are not actually physically present and represent whether or not a creature has a soul, conflicting with SCP-104 on a very basic level.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may change at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, during my read through of this skip as part of my wikiwalk research for the week, the orbs at the end reminded me a lot of SCP-104 in their apparent following of people and constant presence. Whose to say that 104 is not a broken version of one of the orbs from 1344? As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Subjects that do not remove an instance of SCP-1344, report seeing orbs with an average estimated diameter of 8.7 cm, and of varying colouration, hovering in place over the heads of both the majority of human beings and other mammals subjects are exposed to. These orbs move with the persons or animals in question. Tests with various species of insects, arachnids, fish, amphibians, reptiles and birds have yielded no results. It is unknown why this phenomenon is only reported with the majority of human beings as opposed to all other mammals. Research into the exact criteria for the manifestation of this phenomenon is ongoing.

To this:

Subjects that do not remove an instance of SCP-1344, report seeing orbs with an average estimated diameter of 8.7 cm, and of varying colouration, hovering in place over the heads of both the majority of human beings and other mammals subjects are exposed to. These orbs move with the persons or animals in question. Tests with various species of insects, arachnids, fish, amphibians, reptiles and birds have yielded no results. It is unknown why this phenomenon is only reported with the majority of human beings as opposed to all other mammals. Research into the exact criteria for the manifestation of this phenomenon is ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice body horror, but there is not even a hint of what the orbs mean. Or the colors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My reaction after reading this was "What the fuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuck"

Which is good. I was loving it until a comment in the first page that basically explains the things as "souls". Then I stopped loving it =\ but since that explanation wasn't written into the piece, I still upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have seen it with an unjustified -13 when it was new, and I made into -12, because it was anomalous, eye-gorging creepy, and USEFUL, just perfect to watch 173 and 689 instead of simply disposing of D-class.

I really don't see why people thought the original was bad. SCPs don't have all to be evil or deleterious, you know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No problem, thanks for providing me the base to work with. I always liked your original article, so when the chance to rewrite it came up, I jumped on it.

So, now that you're back: write more. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn. It's been a few years since I've even taken a look at the Foundation site, and all I have to say is that if a page I wrote was going to be re-written, I'm glad it was this good. +1 and thanks for saving the goggles, Crayne.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Who knows if its even measuring souls? Is Junior Research Assistant ███████ bald? Maybe its measuring average hair length to height ratio? That would give null results on non-mammals, too. The whole souls thing could just be an inaccurate assumption

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's nice. I like the weird implications that are made through some people not having souls. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, man. Considering I fucked over giraffes in a weird sort of tribute, I'm pleased to hear you like this one. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like this one. Reminds me of something I might write up, but that might just be because of the orb(soul?) thing, and the fact that one of the researchers didn't have one. The squick was carried out well, and it held my interest throughout the article. Take a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sounds like good fodder for the Personnel mugshots thread, if there are any pictures.

And thank you! I'm always pleased there are so many here with a Star Control affinity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for that huge compliment. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You keep having such awesome sounding stories going on behind the scenes. You awesome terrible person, you. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't tell if this is a brilliant sanitization of "It rips your eyes out painfully" or if it's a poorly worded way of saying that it rips your eyes out.

Well, you at least considered the first option, so I'd say I succeeded. ;)

Are these needles barbed? Is that what you're getting at?

It's certainly one possible reason why the goggles can't be taken off without dangly results

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This provokes the expected physical responses.

I can't tell if this is a brilliant sanitization of "It rips your eyes out painfully" or if it's a poorly worded way of saying that it rips your eyes out.

Safe removal of SCP-1344 is only possible through an invasive medical procedure that leaves the patient blind.

Are these needles barbed? Is that what you're getting at?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generic mad scientist. Was fun, though. I never pass up an opportunity to wear my lab coat.

Also: Have some love from one Star Control fan to another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But then, how would we know?!?

And thanks…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just enough here for an upvote. Interesting, a little mysterious, and good job on the body horror. I mean really, go blind or lose your eyeballs? The only remedy is don't put them on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I know it's been done better. This wasn't an attempt to outdo either of those. As I tried to explain, the Junior Research Assistant really isn't the problem; it's this guy "John".

Damn you on your eye-squick-immunity though. Damn you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I don't like this. The whole "SCP reveals that some researchers aren't people" angle has been done better by SCP-1487 and SCP-1719. The addendum is fine and good, but not enough to make me care. Also I'm immune to eye-squick, apparently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a story beyond the narrative. Not sure if it'll get fleshed out, but it's there. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't worry. It doesn't hurt. Putting them on, I mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love these lines. A similar line was my favorite part of the original, and these are somehow even better.

Thanks, those were a very conscious choice.

I'm not terribly intrigued by the orbs, but I can see how paranoid foundation researchers could become obsessed with divining their "meaning."

Indeed. In fact, it's to be seen whether they're actually there or simply a translation of something else by the goggles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Horrible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a set of welding goggles exactly like the ones in that picture, color and all. Got them for a Halloween costume. Aaand now I'm afraid to put them on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHH!

Eye horror always gets me. +1

I would actually like to see more tied to this. Your reference to the research assistant being the motivation aparks my interest in a larger story behind the scenes here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huge improvement to tone here. The note at the end and related comments make me more intrigued about the variety of goggle types, something that bothered me from last round.

…the psychological repercussions are as expected… This provokes the expected physical responses…

Love these lines. A similar line was my favorite part of the original, and these are somehow even better. Likewise love the psychological manipulation of the D-class by enticing them with an improvement of their vision. Also, the clinical talk about dangling, scooped-out eyeballs. Ick.

I'm not terribly intrigued by the orbs, but I can see how paranoid foundation researchers could become obsessed with divining their "meaning."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there is a mole, it's not that guy. I will say he's the reason "John" needs these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing the mole is the soul-lacking Assistant Researcher?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my. This is disturbing, in a good way. Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Not the best, but kept me interested the whole way and has me guessing on the nature of the orbs. That's worth an upvote. I guess the implication is that Junior Assistant Reasearcher Blankity isn't human…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything under this bar just sounds awkward. The fact that he's asking questions instead of making statements regarding uncertainties makes me cringe.

They're the test lead's observations and notes. Perhaps that's not clear enough? Otherwise I'm not sure I understand what you're trying to get across.

Edit: Removed the casual remarks

What's the point of the last two addenda? Am I missing something? There's nothing to hook me here.

+ It collapses or else it gets the hose again
- Might wanna try some lotion on that

Someone recruited a craftsman to produce goggles that can be used to determine certain aspects of people's souls (the fact that animals have them too is secondary). They fucked up in their background check of this individual though, thinking he was reliable to work with. He wasn't. Didn't get that this wasn't some kind of Halloween joke and ended up shipping an anomaly through the post, which then made it end up in Foundation care. Whoever ordered these things, and whatever their plans for them are, they aren't content with loose ends. And they'd probably kill to have a mole inside the Foundation to get their goggles back.

I value your opinion, Faminepulse, so thanks for commenting. If you have specific suggestions for improvements, let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, soul viewer with some eye squick, whatever. Here are my qualms.

Test lead's comments

Everything under this bar just sounds awkward. The fact that he's asking questions instead of making statements regarding uncertainties makes me cringe.

What's the point of the last two addenda? Am I missing something? There's nothing to hook me here.

Otherwise, no vote. If this was the first thing I saw on the wiki it'd probably get me curious, but I know this is a very bland article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I'm rather fond of the stereotypical psychiatrist's approach: what do you think they mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really want to know what the orbs mean, but i guess it's probably better to leave it a mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a complete rewrite of SCP-1344 (original author 182crazyking). I've kept the main concept: eyeball-piercing body horror, but I decided to forego pupil dilation control in favor of a new direction. Hope you enjoy it.

Edit: Soulless, the eyes are yours, 'kay?
